Combat Resolution System

D&D Mechanics Overview

This roleplay uses a D&D-inspired dice resolution system for situations where outcome is contested — primarily combat, but also skill checks, exploration, and social encounters. The system is lightweight and designed to enhance roleplay, not replace it.

Dice are rolled when there is genuine uncertainty about an outcome. If a situation is clearly narrated and uncontested, dice are not required. Use your judgment — dice exist to resolve conflict, not to determine every action.

🎲 The Core Mechanic

Roll 1d20 and add your relevant modifier. Meet or beat the Difficulty Class (DC) set by the moderator or the opposing player. Simple, fast, and flexible.

Core Systems

How Rolls Work

Your Stand's stat grades translate directly into modifier values:

Stat GradeRoll ModifierNotes
S+7Mod-approved only. Essentially superhuman.
A+5Peak human / Stand ability. Very powerful.
B+3Above average. Solid in combat.
C+1Average. Not specialized here.
D-1Weak. Avoid this area in combat.
E-3Critical weakness. Very likely to fail.

When you attempt an action, choose the most relevant stat. Attack rolls use Destructive Power. Evasion rolls use Speed. Precision-based targeting uses Precision. Tanking hits uses Durability.

Difficulty Classes

DC Reference

DifficultyDCExample Situation
Easy8Catching a slow-moving projectile, basic technique
Moderate12Landing a hit on an alert opponent, a complex technique
Hard16Breaking through a defensive ability, a desperate move
Very Hard20Countering a powerful Special, surviving a critical hit
Legendary24+Impossible-tier actions, rarely called for

Special Mechanics

Critical Hits & Fumbles

⚡
Natural 20 — Critical!
Roll a 20 (before modifier) and your action succeeds spectacularly. In combat, deal double damage. In skill checks, achieve something beyond the expected scope.
💥
Natural 1 — Fumble
Roll a 1 and something goes wrong — not catastrophically, but meaningfully. In combat, you might expose yourself. In skill checks, the attempt backfires.
🛡️
Contested Rolls
When both sides roll (attack vs. defense), the higher total wins. Ties go to the defender in physical actions, attacker in special ability conflicts.
🎯
Advantage / Disadvantage
In favorable situations, roll 2d20 and take the higher. In unfavorable situations, roll 2d20 and take the lower. Moderators can grant these contextually.

Stand Abilities

Using Special Abilities

Stand special abilities — time stop, healing, phasing, etc. — follow their own rules rather than raw dice, but dice are used to determine if an ability can be applied in a contested situation. If an opponent has a reasonable counter (high Speed to escape before time stops, for instance), they make a DC 18 Speed roll to avoid the effect.

Abilities that are declared as automatic (like Killer Queen's secondary bombs) have their own activation roll at DC 12 when conditions are met. Abilities that require the Stand to physically touch or reach the target use a contested roll.

Remember: Dice enhance the story — they do not replace good roleplay. A well-written scene with contextual logic will always be weighted favorably by moderators, even against unfavorable rolls.
